Okay, so during the interviewing process, you may have come across candidates that come from bigger companies, meaning large gyms, global gyms, etc. Awesome. However, I do want to go over some of the things that you need to be cautious of in doing that. Okay, it's really easy to get caught up in how much growth you're experiencing right now, and you kind of want to wave your hands in the air, and you're like, I should get someone in here who's more experienced than I am to like take over and do this shit, right? Okay, the thing is, you need to be cautious of a couple of things regarding bringing someone in who has more experience than you are used to. Okay, first one is a cadence mismatch. Okay, what does that mean? That means that in a larger company, in a gym that has a thousand, two thousand members, this person has been conditioned to have everything brought to their attention. Okay, in a bigger company, they don't often have to go digging for information. For example, as a small one, a small example would be that they have a dashboard that shows them all the numbers every day. Almost any global gym has that. Whereas in your company, they're going to have to go find the numbers. There's other people responsible for the numbers, and they might not always be correct. They might have to dig up. They might have to do some math, etc. Right? Okay, and so what this, what can happen in, when that person comes into your company is that it seems like they're not taking initiative or that they're super slow. But the fact of the matter is they're not used to the fact that you don't have that information readily available. And so you need to identify that in the beginning and make it clear that they're going to have to do more work than they were used to just to have those numbers, right? Or whatever it may be. The second is a skill set mismatch. Okay, so running a large gym is an entirely different job than running a small gym. When you run a large club, you tend to be really good at making decisions, organization, process improvement, SOPs, large group communication. Okay? However, when you are building a gym and you are growing a gym, there's not as many complex decisions for a manager to make. There are no processes to improve, and the communication is basic and simple, right? You are in the middle of building, meaning that they have to create processes from scratch. They have to get their hands dirty to find information, and they have to get used to basic communication, meaning it's probably a lot less email. It's a lot less announcements, a lot more one-on-ones meetings, etc. Okay, so here are the two areas that there's typically a mismatch when you are talking to a candidate of higher experience, right? So how do you screen for this in the interview process? Because most of the problems don't stem from this person having more experience. They stem from you not understanding what that means that they're to come in, right? Which is why I felt this was important to make. So how do you screen? A good first question to ask is what will you do in your first month on the job? What you want to be weary of is answers that overemphasize on the learning and training. What that tells you is they think there's like a whole bunch of crap they have to learn that your business is super complex and that, you know, it's going to take a while before they can wrap their head around it. It might come across like this person is slow, mellow, they're not a driver, and that's, you know, something that you obviously don't want. You need someone who's going to drive things forward and who naturally takes initiative. And if they've been conditioned too long from their last job to like wait for people to make decisions, to move slow, to take a long time to learn things, I do not think that's going to be a good match because in a smaller gym you need to be able to move faster, make more decisions, things are going to break faster, and you don't take a month or two months to learn and be trained up, right? Other two questions. One, how will your new job differ from your current job? Okay, what you're looking for is their level of awareness. You want them to point out the fact that they are going to have to create things where in the job that they have today, they don't have to do that. If they're able to point that out and say that they understand that and they're excited for it, then that's great, right? You basically want someone to have insight into the differences between their two jobs, okay? And lastly, asking someone why do you want to join a smaller company, right? And what you really want to look for is somebody who has a desire to create, to drive things forward, to make more decisions and have more influence because that kind of person is going to take initiative. So if you ask all of these questions and you feel good about the answers, I see no problem moving forward with the candidate. The issue stems from not asking these questions and not taking the time to identify possible constraints that you could have with one of these candidates.
